summary: Joel Spolsky argues that venture capital is fundamentally misaligned with the goals of most software entrepreneurs. VCs seek high-risk, high-reward moonshots because they diversify across many investments, while founders prefer higher-probability moderate success since they can't diversify their one company. He explains that VC-funded companies are pressured to grow faster than is healthy, causing imbalances between revenue, hiring, PR, and code quality. Additionally, VCs' filtering process and increasingly harsh deal terms (exploding offers, preferential shares, board control) systematically drive away the smartest founders, leaving VCs with a shrinking pool of desperate or naive companies unlikely to contain the next big success.
key_points: VCs and founders have fundamentally misaligned risk preferences: VCs want low-probability massive returns across a portfolio, while founders want high-probability moderate returns on their single company
Healthy company growth requires keeping revenue, hiring, PR, and code quality roughly in sync — VC pressure to accelerate growth throws these curves out of balance, creating fatal problems
VCs' need to reject 999 out of 1000 pitches creates a whack-a-mole filtering process that optimizes for saying no quickly rather than identifying truly great companies
Increasingly harsh deal terms (exploding offers, preferential shares, founder displacement) systematically repel founders with good business sense, leaving VCs with only desperate or naive companies
The best potential investments are often companies too busy building products and serving customers to play the fundraising game
money_quotes: Founders would prefer reasonable success with high probability, while VCs are looking for fantastic hit-it-out-of-the-ballpark success with low probability.
This is just Econ 101; it's the same reason you buy car insurance and Hertz doesn't.
As an entrepreneur, you're going to be forced at gunpoint to bet on three cherries again and again and again.
They spend all this money before anyone has had a chance to learn what the best way to spend money is.
Nothing sends a stronger message that an offer is uncompetitive than refusing to expose it to competition.
All these tough deals are not really protecting the VCs, they're just restricting the VCs' world of possible investments to dumb companies and desperate companies.
Many excellent entrepreneurs feel that their time is better spent pitching products to customers rather than pitching stock to investors.
It seems to me that a company that accepts an exploding offer is demonstrating a remarkable lack of basic business aptitude.
key_insight: The VC model's misaligned incentives, rushed filtering, and increasingly harsh deal terms create a self-defeating cycle that systematically drives away the best founders and companies, leaving VCs fishing in an ever-shrinking pool unlikely to contain breakthrough successes.
